exercises.title: The four orchestration patterns
exercises.prompt: Four orchestration patterns recur across 2026 frameworks. Name them (supervisor-worker, swarm/peer-to-peer, hierarchical, debate) and when each fits. State Anthropic's 'build the right system' rule and how it gates topology choice.
exercises.hint: Supervisor-worker: a coordinator routes to workers. Swarm: peers hand off directly. Hierarchical: nested (subgraphs/teams of teams). Debate: agents converge via critique. Anthropic's rule: build the right system for YOUR needs — start simple (one agent + workflows), add topology only when insufficient.
exercises.solution:
**The four recurring orchestration patterns:**
- **Supervisor-worker:** a **coordinator agent routes subtasks to worker agents** and synthesizes results (centralized control, Lesson 13). *Fits when:* the task **decomposes into subtasks a coordinator can route** (research → search/write/review workers), and you want **clear central control and accountability**. The most common, easiest-to-reason-about pattern.
- **Swarm / peer-to-peer:** agents **hand off directly to each other as peers** (no central coordinator) — a chain of specialist hand-offs (Lesson 13). *Fits when:* the workflow is a **sequence of specialist hand-offs** where each agent knows who goes next (triage → specialist), no central coordination needed. More flexible/emergent, harder to reason about globally.
- **Hierarchical:** **nested structure** — teams of teams, subgraphs within graphs (Lesson 13). *Fits when:* the task has **natural hierarchy/composition** — a high-level workflow where some steps are themselves complex multi-step sub-workflows. Enables modular composition of complex systems.
- **Debate:** multiple agents **propose and critique each other to converge** on a better answer (Lesson 25). *Fits when:* **high-stakes factuality/reasoning** where consensus/cross-examination improves the answer and you can afford the N×R cost.

**Anthropic's 'build the right system' rule:** Anthropic's guidance — **'It's about building the right system for your needs'** — means **don't pick a topology for its own sake; pick what your problem actually requires.** And critically: **start simple (a single agent plus the five workflow patterns, Lesson 12) and add multi-agent topology ONLY when that's insufficient.** 

**How it gates topology choice:** the rule **gates** topology behind genuine need:
- **Default: a single agent + workflow patterns (Lesson 12)** — most tasks are solvable with **one agent and the five workflow patterns** (prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, evaluator-optimizer). This is the **simplest, most reliable, cheapest** option.
- **Only escalate to multi-agent topology when a single agent + workflows genuinely can't do it** — e.g. the task needs true parallelism across specialists (supervisor/swarm), natural hierarchy (hierarchical), or consensus on hard problems (debate). 
- **Match the topology to the problem's shape** (Phase 11.17) — supervisor for central routing, swarm for hand-off chains, hierarchical for nested complexity, debate for consensus — but **only after** confirming a simpler approach is insufficient.
The rule is the phase's **'simple over complex' principle** (Lessons 2, 12, 15, 30) applied to **topology**: **multi-agent orchestration adds complexity, cost, and failure modes** (MASFT, Lesson 26 — inter-agent misalignment, coordination failures) — so **don't reach for it until a single agent + workflows demonstrably can't solve the problem.** Topology is a **last resort, gated by need**, not a default. 'Build the right system for your needs' = use the **simplest topology that works**, and most of the time that's **no multi-agent topology at all** (one agent + workflows).

exercises.title: Tool-call-based supervision vs supervisor libraries
exercises.prompt: Describe the 2026 LangChain recommendation: tool-call-based supervision vs dedicated supervisor libraries. Implement supervisor orchestration where the supervisor routes to workers via tool calls. Why is tool-call-based supervision often preferred over a heavy supervisor abstraction?
exercises.hint: Tool-call-based: model the workers as TOOLS the supervisor calls (transfer_to_X / call_worker) — reuse the function-calling machinery, no special framework. Supervisor libraries: dedicated abstractions for supervisor-worker. Tool-call preferred: simpler, no new mechanism, the model already knows tool-calling, less framework lock-in.
exercises.solution:
**The 2026 LangChain recommendation (tool-call-based vs supervisor libraries):**
- **Tool-call-based supervision:** model the **worker agents as tools the supervisor calls** — the supervisor invokes `call_research_worker(...)` or `transfer_to_writer(...)` as **ordinary tool/function calls** (the handoff-as-tool pattern, Lesson 16). No special supervisor framework — you **reuse the standard function-calling machinery** (Phase 13.1). The supervisor is just an agent whose 'tools' happen to be other agents.
- **Supervisor libraries:** **dedicated framework abstractions** for supervisor-worker orchestration — purpose-built supervisor classes/constructs that manage routing, worker lifecycle, result aggregation.
The 2026 LangChain recommendation **leans toward tool-call-based supervision** for many cases — model workers as tools rather than reaching for a heavy supervisor abstraction.

**Implementing tool-call-based supervisor orchestration:**
```python
def supervisor_orchestration(supervisor_agent, workers):
    # Expose each worker as a TOOL the supervisor can call
    worker_tools = {f"call_{name}": (lambda task, w=w: w.run(task))
                    for name, w in workers.items()}
    def supervise(goal):
        # The supervisor is just an agent whose tools ARE the workers
        return supervisor_agent.run(goal, tools=worker_tools)
    return supervise

# The supervisor reasons, decides which worker to call (a tool call),
# the worker runs, returns its result (an observation), the supervisor
# continues — the standard agent loop (Lesson 1), workers as tools.
```
The supervisor **runs the normal agent loop** (Lesson 1) — it reasons about the goal, **decides which worker to call (a tool call)**, the worker executes and returns a result (the observation), and the supervisor continues until done. **Workers are just tools** in the supervisor's tool registry — no special orchestration framework needed.

**Why tool-call-based supervision is often preferred:**
1. **Simplicity — no new mechanism:** workers-as-tools **reuses the function-calling machinery the model already knows** (Phase 13.1, Lesson 16's handoff-as-tool). There's **no special supervisor abstraction to learn** — it's just an agent calling tools (that happen to be agents). The model already excels at tool-calling, so routing-via-tool-call works naturally.
2. **Less framework lock-in / complexity:** a **dedicated supervisor library** imposes its own abstraction, lifecycle, and conventions (Phase 11.17's framework-leak risk — the abstraction can fight you). **Tool-call-based supervision uses primitives you already have** (agents + tools), so it's **lighter, more flexible, and less locked-in** — you're not committing to a heavy supervisor framework.
3. **Composability:** since workers are tools, you can **mix them with other tools**, swap them, and compose freely — the standard tool-registry flexibility (Phase 13.5).
4. **The model handles the routing:** the supervisor's **reasoning + tool-selection** (Phase 13.5) *is* the routing logic — the model decides which worker (tool) to call, which is what it's good at. A supervisor library often just wraps this anyway.
The preference reflects the phase's **'simple over complex'** principle (Lesson 12, Anthropic's rule, Exercise 1): **use the lightweight primitive (workers-as-tools) rather than a heavy framework abstraction (supervisor library) unless the abstraction genuinely earns its complexity.** Tool-call-based supervision gives you supervisor-worker orchestration with **no new machinery, less lock-in, and the model's native tool-calling doing the routing** — simpler and often sufficient. Reach for a dedicated supervisor library only when you need its specific features (complex lifecycle management, sophisticated aggregation) that tool-call-based supervision doesn't easily provide. (Same lesson as OpenAI Agents SDK's handoffs-as-tools, Lesson 16 — model control transfer as tool calls, not a separate mechanism.)

exercises.title: The topology-complexity gradient, when each pattern earns its cost, and the simple-first doctrine
exercises.prompt: Explain the complexity gradient from single-agent to multi-agent topologies, why each step up adds cost and failure modes (MASFT, Lesson 26), how to decide when a topology earns its complexity, and why 'start simple, add topology only when insufficient' is the doctrine across the whole phase.
exercises.hint: Gradient: single agent + workflows → supervisor → swarm/hierarchical → debate (increasing complexity/cost). Each step adds: more LLM calls (cost), coordination/inter-agent failure modes (MASFT), harder debugging. Topology earns cost only when it solves something a simpler shape can't. Doctrine: complexity is a liability; default simple.
exercises.solution:
**The complexity gradient (single-agent → multi-agent topologies):**
```
single agent + workflows (Lesson 12)   ← simplest, cheapest, most reliable
  → supervisor-worker                   (central routing, more agents)
  → swarm / hierarchical                (peer hand-offs / nesting — more coordination)
  → debate                              (N×R agents — most expensive)
                                        → increasing complexity, cost, failure surface
```
Each step up the gradient **adds capability** (parallelism, specialization, consensus) **but also adds complexity, cost, and failure modes.**

**Why each step up adds cost and failure modes (MASFT, Lesson 26):**
- **Cost:** more agents = **more LLM calls** (each agent runs its own loop) — supervisor adds the coordinator's calls, swarm adds hand-off overhead, debate multiplies by N×R (Lesson 25). Multi-agent is **expensive** (Phase 11.11's economics, multiplied).
- **Inter-agent failure modes (MASFT, Lesson 26):** multi-agent introduces **whole new failure categories** that a single agent doesn't have — **inter-agent misalignment** (agents working at cross-purposes, miscommunication), **coordination failures** (the supervisor mis-routes, hand-offs drop information), and **error propagation between agents** (one agent's bad output corrupts another's). MASFT's 14 failure modes are largely **multi-agent-specific** — adding agents adds these failure modes.
- **Harder debugging/observability:** more agents = **more complex traces, harder to reason about the whole system** (the actor-model's no-global-view problem, Lesson 14; distributed-system complexity).
- **More to get right:** roles, routing, hand-offs, aggregation, coordination — each is a place to fail (Phase 11.17's abstraction leaks).
So **each step up the gradient is a real cost** — more money, more failure modes, more complexity. The capability gain must **justify** these costs.

**How to decide when a topology earns its complexity:** a topology **earns its complexity only when it solves something a simpler shape genuinely can't:**
1. **Start at the bottom (single agent + workflows, Lesson 12)** and **try to solve the problem there first.**
2. **Identify the specific limitation** that makes the simple approach insufficient — e.g. 'I need genuine parallelism across specialists that one agent can't provide' (→ supervisor/swarm), 'the task has irreducible nested complexity' (→ hierarchical), 'this hard problem needs consensus/cross-examination for factuality' (→ debate).
3. **Add the minimum topology that addresses that specific limitation** — and **only that** (don't over-engineer).
4. **Verify the topology actually helps** — measure (eval-driven, Lesson 30) that the added complexity **improves the outcome enough to justify the cost and failure surface.** If it doesn't measurably help, **drop back to the simpler shape.**
The test: **'does this topology solve a problem the simpler approach can't, by enough to justify the added cost/complexity/failure-modes?'** If yes, it earns its complexity; if no (or if you haven't confirmed the simpler approach fails), **don't add it.**

**Why 'start simple, add topology only when insufficient' is the doctrine across the whole phase:** this is the **single most repeated principle** in Phase 14 (and the course) — it appears as:
- **Lesson 2 (ReWOO):** plan-then-execute (simple) over interleaved when predictable.
- **Lesson 12 (Anthropic):** workflows over agents; start with direct calls.
- **Lesson 15 (CrewAI):** 'start with a Flow' (deterministic) for production.
- **Lesson 28 (this lesson):** 'build the right system for your needs'; start simple, add topology only when needed.
- **Lesson 30 (Eval-driven):** 'add multi-step agentic systems only when needed.'
It's the doctrine because **complexity is a liability, not an asset:**
- **Every added layer (topology, agent, abstraction) adds cost, failure modes, and debugging difficulty** (the gradient above, MASFT, Lesson 26) — complexity makes systems **less reliable, more expensive, harder to operate.**
- **Most problems don't need it** — a single well-engineered agent + workflows solves the majority of tasks **more reliably and cheaply** than a complex multi-agent topology.
- **Reliability comes from simplicity** — fewer moving parts, fewer failure modes, easier verification (the workbench, Lessons 31-42, is about reliability through engineering, not through more agents).
So the doctrine: **default to the simplest shape (one agent + workflows), and treat every step up the complexity gradient as a cost that must be *justified* by a problem the simpler shape genuinely can't solve — verified by measurement.** Don't add topology because it's powerful or impressive; add it **only when forced to, and the minimum needed.** This is the mature engineering judgment that runs through the entire phase: **agent complexity is a liability to be minimized, not a feature to be maximized** — the right system is the **simplest one that works**, and complexity is added reluctantly, by demonstrated need, and removed when it doesn't earn its keep. The whole phase — from the agent loop (Lesson 1) through frameworks, orchestration, and the workbench — circles back to this: **simple, well-engineered, verified agents beat complex, under-engineered ones**, and topology is the last thing you add, not the first.
